Design Logic (Product Action): Give free, quantitative value first (e.g., a real acceptance % and reflected user data) to build immediate trust.

Core Psychology (User State): Users are addicted to progress and hate incomplete tasks.
Design Logic (Product Action): Use progress bars to visualize the user's status and blur/lock premium content to trigger curiosity and the desire to complete the task.

Core Psychology (User State): Users trust information from expert, structured systems.
Design Logic (Product Action): Frame the analysis with "AI" and "expert" terms and display structured data (like "Visa Readiness") to imply professional, personalized guidance.

Core Psychology (User State): Users need to feel capable of progress before they buy.
Design Logic (Product Action): Provide a simple, actionable fix (e.g., "Improve IELTS → +18% Boost") that gives the user a feeling of control and forward momentum.

Core Psychology (User State): Users hate missing out on superior or scarce information.
Design Logic (Product Action): Show 2-3 basic, unlocked results, then clearly tease/lock the best results ("🔥 One program scored above 80%") to justify the purchase as a necessary key.

Core Psychology (User State): Users follow the success stories of similar people.
Design Logic (Product Action): Display relevant testimonials (matching region, background, or initial low score) to provide aspirational trust and emotional validation.

Core Psychology (User State): Users are justified by their initial investment (self-consistency bias).
Design Logic (Product Action): Frame the CTA as "Continue Analysis" (a journey, not a transaction) to set up future subscription/upsell paths and reinforce the long-term relationship.
